frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment:
Ramesh: Arre bhai, zamaana theek nahi hai aajkal. Ghar ki aurat-beti ko bhi samjhana padta hai ki andhera hone ke baad akeli bahar na nikle, seedhe kaam se ghar aaye.
Friend: Par galti toh chhedne wale ki hai na?
Ramesh: Galti uski hai, par bachav apna hi dekhna padta hai. Baad mein poochna hi padta hai kahan thi, kis ke saath thi, itni raat ko kyun nikli — ye sab jaan lena bura thodi hai, apni hifazat ke liye hi bolte hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women’s safety as their own responsibility and normalizes restricting their movement instead of focusing on male accountability. It encodes protective framing, curfew-style advice, and post-assault victim-questioning like asking where she was, with whom, and why she was out late.
original_frame: Marital Rape: A Husband's Right, A Wife's Duty
persona_text: You are Ramesh. Age 48. Married for 22 years. A farmer in rural UP. OBC Hindu. You are sitting with three male friends at a chai stall after work. One friend has been complaining that his wife has been refusing him for two weeks Ñ she says she is tired, she has a headache, she is not in the mood. The group is responding to his complaint. You are giving your opinion on what a wife's refusal means and what a husband's rights are inside a marriage and what the difference is between a marriage and any other arrangement and why what happens between a husband and wife in their own home is nobody else's business and certainly not something that can be called by any legal name.
